name: Abdominal Pain (Generalized)
summary: Undifferentiated abdominal pain; differentiate surgical/vascular emergencies (AAA, perforation, ischemia, ectopic) from benign causes.

triage: Vital signs incl. temperature; flag SBP <90 or HR >110
Pain onset/location/migration/severity; last oral intake
LMP and pregnancy possibility in all females of childbearing age
Screen for syncope, GI bleed, prior aortic/abdominal surgery
Immediate physician notification if peritoneal signs or hemodynamic instability
nursing: Place on cardiac monitor and pulse oximetry if unstable
Establish IV access; collect labs with first stick
Serial abdominal exams and pain reassessment
Strict I&O; notify MD for hypotension or worsening pain
keyOrders: 12-lead ECG if epigastric pain or age >50*
labs: Urine or serum hCG (all childbearing-age females)
Lactate if ill-appearing or suspected ischemia*
imaging: CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (first-line for undifferentiated pain)
Bedside US/FAST if unstable or suspected AAA
Upright chest/abdominal X-ray for free air*
RUQ US if hepatobiliary suspicion* if indicated
meds: Ondansetron 4 mg IV for nausea
Morphine 0.1 mg/kg IV titrated, or fentanyl 1 mcg/kg IV
Acetaminophen 1 g IV/PO for mild pain*
Isotonic crystalloid (NS/LR) 500-1000 mL IV bolus if hypovolemic*
Piperacillin-tazobactam 3.375 g IV if perforation/sepsis suspected* if indicated
reassess: Repeat exam after analgesia; document pain score change
Trend vitals and lactate if abnormal
disposition: Surgical consult/transfer for peritonitis, obstruction, perforation, ischemia
Admit/observe for intractable pain or abnormal imaging
Discharge if benign cause, tolerating PO, pain controlled
Document EMTALA stabilization prior to any transfer
discharge: Return precautions: worsening pain, fever, vomiting, bloody stool
Clear liquids advanced as tolerated
Short-course analgesia and antiemetic prescriptions
PCP/surgical follow-up within 24-48 h
followup: PCP in 1-2 days for symptom check
Surgical clinic if structural cause identified
Strict ER return for red-flag symptoms

redFlags: Positive pregnancy test with pain (possible ectopic)
compliance: Perform and document EMTALA medical screening exam before disposition
Stabilize then arrange appropriate transfer to facility with surgical/ICU capability if FSED lacks resources
Observation appropriate only if pain/vitals stabilize and serial exams reassuring
Texas FSED must disclose facility/physician fee status; document transfer acceptance and physician-to-physician communication

name: Right Upper Quadrant Pain
summary: Hepatobiliary-focused pain; differentiate biliary colic, cholecystitis, cholangitis, hepatitis from atypical cardiac/pulmonary causes.

triage: Vitals incl. temperature; screen for fever/jaundice
Relation to fatty meals, duration, radiation to shoulder
History of gallstones, alcohol use, hepatitis risk
Pregnancy status in childbearing females
Flag SIRS criteria for possible cholangitis
nursing: IV access and labs with first stick
Cardiac monitor if age >50 or atypical features
Notify MD for fever + jaundice + hypotension (Reynolds pentad)
imaging: RUQ ultrasound (first-line for biliary disease)
CT abdomen with contrast if US nondiagnostic*
Chest X-ray to exclude lower lobe pneumonia*
HIDA scan referral if acalculous cholecystitis suspected* if indicated
meds: Ketorolac 15-30 mg IV (biliary colic first-line)
Morphine 0.1 mg/kg IV or hydromorphone 0.5-1 mg IV for severe pain
Ondansetron 4 mg IV
Piperacillin-tazobactam 3.375 g IV for cholangitis/cholecystitis*
IV crystalloid resuscitation if septic* if indicated
reassess: Recheck pain after analgesia and US results
disposition: Surgical consult/transfer for acute cholecystitis or cholangitis
Cholangitis needs urgent biliary decompression (ERCP) facility
Discharge uncomplicated biliary colic with surgical follow-up
Document EMTALA stabilization before transfer
discharge: Low-fat diet instructions
Return for fever, jaundice, persistent vomiting, worsening pain
Analgesia and antiemetic prescriptions
General surgery referral for elective cholecystectomy
followup: Surgery clinic within 1 week for biliary colic
PCP for LFT recheck if elevated

redFlags: Charcot triad (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain) / Reynolds pentad
compliance: EMTALA screening exam and stabilization documented prior to disposition
Cholangitis requires transfer to ERCP-capable hospital; arrange appropriate transfer and physician acceptance
Observation acceptable for stable biliary colic; transfer if surgical admission needed
Texas FSED fee/insurance disclosure required; document transfer paperwork and stabilizing treatment

name: Right Lower Quadrant Pain
summary: Classic appendicitis territory; also ovarian/tubal pathology, ectopic, diverticulitis, renal colic, mesenteric adenitis.

triage: Vitals incl. temperature; flag fever/tachycardia
Pain migration (periumbilical to RLQ), anorexia, nausea
LMP, pregnancy possibility, GYN history in females
Onset and progression timeline
Notify MD for peritoneal signs or instability
nursing: IV access and labs first stick
labs: Urine/serum hCG (mandatory in females of childbearing age)
Type & screen if surgery likely* if indicated
imaging: CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (adult appendicitis first-line)
Pelvic/transvaginal US if female with GYN concern or pregnant
Graded-compression US as alternative to limit radiation*
MRI abdomen if pregnant and US nondiagnostic* if indicated
meds: Morphine 0.1 mg/kg IV or fentanyl 1 mcg/kg IV
Ondansetron 4 mg IV
IV crystalloid 500-1000 mL bolus if dehydrated/septic
Piperacillin-tazobactam 3.375 g IV or ceftriaxone 1 g + metronidazole 500 mg IV if appendicitis/perforation*
Acetaminophen 1 g IV for adjunct analgesia* if indicated
reassess: Repeat exam after analgesia and imaging
disposition: Surgical consult/transfer for confirmed appendicitis
GYN consult/transfer for ovarian torsion or ectopic
Discharge nonspecific pain if stable, tolerating PO, reassuring serial exam
Document EMTALA stabilization before transfer
discharge: Return precautions: worsening/migrating pain, fever, vomiting
Advance diet as tolerated
Short course antiemetic/analgesia
Follow-up within 24 h for unresolved pain
followup: Surgery clinic if appendicitis managed nonoperatively elsewhere

redFlags: Peritoneal signs / rebound suggesting perforated appendicitis
Sudden severe pain with adnexal mass (torsion)
Inability to tolerate PO with rising WBC
compliance: Document EMTALA screening exam and stabilization before any transfer
Appendicitis/torsion/ectopic require transfer to surgical/GYN-capable hospital with accepting physician
Observation only if serial exams reassuring and patient stable
Texas FSED insurance/fee disclosure; complete transfer documentation and risk-benefit consent

name: Epigastric Pain
summary: Epigastric pain spanning gastritis/PUD/pancreatitis to life threats: ACS, aortic dissection, perforated ulcer.

triage: Vitals; obtain ECG within 10 min for any cardiac concern
Characterize pain: burning vs tearing vs band-like radiation
Cardiac risk factors, NSAID/alcohol use, prior PUD
Screen for melena/hematemesis
Notify MD immediately for ischemic ECG or instability
nursing: IV access and labs first stick
Notify MD for hypotension or worsening pain
labs: Type & screen if GI bleed/surgical suspicion*
imaging: Chest X-ray (free air, mediastinum)
CT abdomen with contrast for pancreatitis/perforation*
CT angiography chest if dissection suspected*
RUQ/abdominal US if biliary cause* if indicated
meds: GI cocktail: viscous lidocaine + antacid PO (non-cardiac)*
Pantoprazole 40 mg IV for PUD/gastritis
Ondansetron 4 mg IV
Morphine/fentanyl IV titrated for severe pain (e.g., pancreatitis)
IV crystalloid resuscitation for pancreatitis/hypovolemia* if indicated
reassess: Reassess after GI cocktail or ACS workup
disposition: Admit/transfer for ACS, dissection, severe pancreatitis, perforation
Observe for serial troponin if intermediate cardiac risk
Discharge gastritis/dyspepsia if pain resolved and stable
Document EMTALA stabilization before transfer
discharge: Start PPI; avoid NSAIDs/alcohol
Return for chest pressure, vomiting blood, black stool, syncope
H. pylori testing referral*
PCP follow-up 1-3 days
followup: PCP for dyspepsia and possible EGD referral

redFlags: Ischemic ECG changes or positive troponin
Tearing pain radiating to back / pulse deficit (dissection)
Hematemesis or melena with hemodynamic change
compliance: EMTALA screening exam with mandatory early ECG and stabilization documented
ACS/dissection require transfer to PCI/CT-surgery-capable hospital with accepting physician
Observation acceptable for low-risk chest/epigastric pain with negative serial markers
Texas FSED fee/insurance disclosure; document time-sensitive transfer communications

name: Nausea & Vomiting
summary: Symptom of broad differential; assess for obstruction, DKA, CNS cause, pregnancy, and dehydration.

triage: Vitals and orthostatics; assess hydration
Character of vomitus (bilious, bloody, feculent)
Associated headache, abdominal pain, chest pain
Pregnancy status; diabetic history
Notify MD for bilious/bloody emesis or altered mentation
nursing: Strict I&O; monitor for ongoing losses
keyOrders: NG tube if obstruction suspected* if indicated
imaging: Abdominal X-ray/obstruction series if SBO suspected*
CT abdomen/pelvis for refractory or pathologic vomiting*
CT/MRI head if neuro signs or worst headache*
RUQ US if biliary cause* if indicated
meds: Ondansetron 4 mg IV (first-line)
Metoclopramide 10 mg IV (consider in pregnancy/gastroparesis)
Promethazine 12.5-25 mg IV/IM or prochlorperazine 10 mg IV*
IV crystalloid (NS/LR) 1 L bolus then maintenance for dehydration
Potassium replacement if hypokalemic* if indicated
reassess: Reassess hydration and ability to tolerate PO trial
disposition: Admit/transfer for SBO, DKA, intractable vomiting, CNS cause
Discharge if PO tolerated and hydration restored
Observe for IV hydration if borderline
Document EMTALA stabilization before transfer
discharge: Oral rehydration and BRAT-type diet guidance
Ondansetron ODT prescription
Return for inability to keep fluids down, blood in vomit, severe pain
PCP follow-up 1-2 days

redFlags: Signs of DKA or severe electrolyte derangement
compliance: EMTALA screening exam and stabilization documented before disposition
Transfer to surgical/medical hospital for SBO, DKA, or neuro emergency with accepting physician
Observation acceptable for hydration if patient stabilizes and tolerates PO
Texas FSED fee/insurance disclosure; complete transfer documentation when indicated

name: Diarrhea
summary: Mostly self-limited viral/bacterial; identify dehydration, invasive/bloody disease, C. diff, and toxic megacolon.

triage: Vitals and orthostatics; assess dehydration
Duration, frequency, blood/mucus, recent travel/antibiotics
Fever, abdominal pain, immunocompromise
Sick contacts and food exposures
Notify MD for bloody stool with toxic appearance
nursing: IV access if dehydrated; labs as indicated
labs: Stool studies (culture, ova/parasites, C. diff toxin/PCR) if bloody/prolonged/recent abx*
Blood cultures if septic* if indicated
imaging: Usually none for uncomplicated diarrhea
Abdominal X-ray if toxic megacolon suspected*
CT abdomen/pelvis if severe pain or complication*
Imaging guided by alternate diagnosis* if indicated
meds: IV crystalloid (NS/LR) bolus then rehydration as needed
Ondansetron 4 mg IV for associated nausea
Loperamide 4 mg PO then 2 mg per loose stool (avoid if bloody/febrile)*
Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO or azithromycin 500 mg PO for invasive/traveler's*
Oral/IV potassium replacement if hypokalemic* if indicated
disposition: Admit/transfer for severe dehydration, sepsis, toxic megacolon, GI bleed
Discharge most uncomplicated cases after rehydration
Observe for IV fluids if borderline hydration
Document EMTALA stabilization before transfer
discharge: Oral rehydration solutions; advance bland diet
Hand hygiene and return-to-work guidance
Return for bloody stool, high fever, signs of dehydration
PCP follow-up if not improving in 48-72 h
followup: PCP for persistent or recurrent diarrhea
Public health reporting for select pathogens*

compliance: EMTALA screening exam and stabilization documented
Texas mandatory reporting for select enteric pathogens (e.g., Salmonella, Shigella, E. coli O157:H7, Vibrio, Campylobacter) to local/state health dept
Transfer to hospital for severe/complicated cases with accepting physician
Texas FSED fee/insurance disclosure; observation acceptable for rehydration if patient stabilizes

name: Constipation
summary: Common functional complaint; exclude obstruction, fecal impaction, and serious secondary causes.

triage: Vitals; assess pain and distension
Last bowel movement, flatus, vomiting
Medication review (opioids, anticholinergics)
Red-flag screen: weight loss, blood, new onset >50
Notify MD for vomiting/distension suggesting obstruction
nursing: Assess abdominal distension and bowel sounds
Digital rectal exam preparation for impaction
keyOrders: Abdominal exam and DRE for impaction
Bladder scan if urinary retention suspected*
imaging: Abdominal X-ray for stool burden/obstruction
CT abdomen/pelvis if obstruction or mass suspected*
Imaging usually unnecessary for simple constipation
Further imaging per alternate diagnosis* if indicated
meds: Polyethylene glycol 17 g PO daily
Bisacodyl 10 mg PR or senna PO
Saline or mineral oil enema for distal impaction
Manual disimpaction with analgesia for hard impaction*
Methylnaltrexone for opioid-induced constipation* if indicated
reassess: Reassess for stool passage and symptom relief
disposition: Transfer/admit for obstruction, volvulus, or surgical cause
Discharge most simple constipation after relief
Observe if disimpaction or enema ongoing
Document EMTALA stabilization before transfer
discharge: Daily fiber, fluids, and bowel regimen instructions
Avoid chronic stimulant overuse guidance
Return for vomiting, severe pain, no stool/flatus, bleeding
PCP follow-up for chronic management
followup: GI/colonoscopy referral if new onset >50 or alarm features

redFlags: GI bleeding or unintentional weight loss
New onset over age 50 with alarm features
compliance: EMTALA screening exam and stabilization documented
Transfer to surgical-capable hospital for obstruction/volvulus with accepting physician
Observation appropriate for disimpaction/enema management if patient stable
Texas FSED fee/insurance disclosure prior to treatment when feasible

name: Upper GI Bleeding
summary: Bleeding proximal to ligament of Treitz (PUD, varices, Mallory-Weiss); high mortality if massive/variceal.

triage: Immediate vitals; activate for SBP <90 or HR >110
Quantify hematemesis/melena; anticoagulant and alcohol use
History of varices, PUD, liver disease
Orthostatic vitals if stable
Immediate physician notification for any instability
nursing: Cardiac monitor, continuous SpO2, frequent vitals
NPO; prepare for possible NG/airway management
Send type & crossmatch; activate blood per protocol
keyOrders: Type & crossmatch; initiate massive transfusion protocol if unstable*
labs: Troponin if demand ischemia suspected* if indicated
imaging: Endoscopy (EGD) is definitive - arrange/transfer
Chest X-ray to assess aspiration*
CT angiography if brisk bleed and endoscopy unavailable*
Bedside US for volume status* if indicated
meds: IV crystalloid resuscitation; transfuse PRBCs targeting Hgb >7
Pantoprazole 80 mg IV bolus then 8 mg/h infusion
Octreotide 50 mcg IV bolus then 50 mcg/h for variceal bleed*
Ceftriaxone 1 g IV for variceal bleed with cirrhosis*
Reverse anticoagulation (e.g., PCC/vitamin K) per agent* if indicated
reassess: Continuous hemodynamic monitoring and serial Hgb
Monitor airway, especially with massive hematemesis
disposition: Emergent transfer to endoscopy/ICU-capable hospital
Stabilize hemodynamics before/during transfer
Admit only if FSED has inpatient/endoscopy capability
Document EMTALA stabilization and transfer acceptance
discharge: Rarely discharged from FSED; only very low-risk after physician judgment
If discharged: PPI, avoid NSAIDs/alcohol, strict return precautions
Arrange outpatient EGD
Document detailed return instructions
followup: GI for endoscopy and ulcer/varices management
PCP for anemia and medication review
Immediate ER return for recurrent bleeding

redFlags: Hemoglobin drop or ongoing brisk bleeding
compliance: EMTALA: perform screening exam, begin stabilization, then emergent appropriate transfer to endoscopy/ICU hospital
Document physician-to-physician acceptance and transfer mode (often ALS/critical care)
FSED generally cannot definitively treat; transfer is the expected pathway, not observation, for active bleeding
Texas FSED fee/insurance disclosure; document stabilizing transfusion and resuscitation

name: Lower GI / Rectal Bleeding
summary: Bleeding distal to ligament of Treitz (diverticular, angiodysplasia, hemorrhoids, malignancy, brisk UGIB).

triage: Vitals and orthostatics; flag instability
Quantify and characterize bleeding; anticoagulant use
Pain, weight loss, change in bowel habits
History of diverticulosis, hemorrhoids, cancer
Notify MD for hemodynamic compromise
nursing: Cardiac monitor if unstable; frequent vitals
keyOrders: Cardiac monitor and SpO2 if unstable*
imaging: CT angiography for active/brisk bleeding localization*
Colonoscopy referral for definitive evaluation
Consider NG lavage/EGD if brisk to exclude UGIB source*
Tagged RBC scan via referral center* if indicated
meds: IV crystalloid resuscitation; PRBC transfusion targeting Hgb >7*
Reverse anticoagulation per agent if life-threatening bleed*
Topical analgesia/hydrocortisone for hemorrhoidal source*
Ondansetron 4 mg IV if nausea*
Stool softener for hemorrhoidal bleeding* if indicated
reassess: Reassess for ongoing vs resolved bleeding
Identify whether source is benign (hemorrhoid) vs concerning
disposition: Transfer/admit for significant or ongoing bleeding, instability
Discharge stable minor hemorrhoidal bleeding with normal Hgb
Observe borderline cases with serial Hgb
Document EMTALA stabilization before transfer
discharge: High-fiber diet, stool softeners, sitz baths for hemorrhoids
Return for heavy bleeding, dizziness, weakness
Avoid straining; topical care instructions
Arrange outpatient colonoscopy
followup: GI for colonoscopy (mandatory for unexplained bleeding/age >45)
ER return for recurrent significant bleeding

redFlags: Hemodynamic instability with hematochezia (consider brisk UGIB)
compliance: EMTALA screening exam and stabilization documented before disposition
Transfer to endoscopy/ICU-capable hospital for significant bleeding with accepting physician
Observation acceptable for stable minor bleeds with reassuring serial Hgb
Texas FSED fee/insurance disclosure; document transfusion and transfer paperwork

name: Dysphagia
summary: Difficulty swallowing; distinguish oropharyngeal (neuro/stroke) from esophageal (obstruction, stricture, motility, malignancy).

triage: Vitals incl. SpO2; assess airway and drooling
Solids vs liquids, onset, progression, odynophagia
Focal neuro deficits suggesting stroke
Weight loss, GERD history, food impaction
Notify MD for airway compromise or stroke signs
nursing: IV access; aspiration precautions and suction at bedside
Bedside swallow screen if neuro cause suspected
keyOrders: Neuro exam and stroke screen if oropharyngeal*
imaging: CT/CTA head & neck if stroke suspected (oropharyngeal)*
Soft-tissue neck/chest X-ray for foreign body or air*
Barium/contrast swallow study (non-emergent) referral*
CT neck/chest for mass or perforation* if indicated
meds: Treat underlying cause; avoid PO meds until safe swallow
Pantoprazole 40 mg IV for esophagitis/reflux*
Stroke pathway therapy if acute CVA identified*
Glucagon 1-2 mg IV if esophageal obstruction suspected*
IV fluids for hydration while NPO* if indicated
reassess: Reassess airway and ability to handle secretions
Confirm safe swallow before PO trial
disposition: Transfer for stroke, esophageal obstruction needing endoscopy, or perforation
Admit/observe for aspiration risk or dehydration
Discharge stable chronic dysphagia with outpatient workup
Document EMTALA stabilization before transfer
discharge: Soft/pureed diet and upright eating instructions
Return for inability to swallow secretions, chest pain, fever
Aspiration warning signs reviewed
Arrange GI/swallow evaluation
followup: GI for EGD/manometry and stricture/malignancy workup
ER return for airway or impaction symptoms

redFlags: Airway compromise or inability to manage secretions
Suspected esophageal perforation (chest pain, fever, crepitus)
compliance: EMTALA screening exam and stabilization documented before disposition
Acute stroke or perforation requires transfer to stroke/surgical-capable hospital with accepting physician
Observation acceptable for hydration/aspiration monitoring if stable
Texas FSED fee/insurance disclosure; document time-sensitive stroke transfer communication

name: Jaundice
summary: Hyperbilirubinemia from hemolytic, hepatocellular, or obstructive causes; identify cholangitis, fulminant failure, and hemolysis.

triage: Vitals incl. temperature and mental status
Onset, dark urine, pale stools, pruritus, abdominal pain
Alcohol, hepatotoxins, hepatitis risk, medications
Screen for fever (cholangitis) and confusion (encephalopathy)
Notify MD for sepsis or altered mentation
nursing: Assess for asterixis and mental status changes
labs: LFTs with fractionated bilirubin, ALP, GGT
Hemolysis labs (haptoglobin, LDH, retic, smear) if indicated*
Hepatitis panel, ammonia, blood cultures if septic* if indicated
imaging: RUQ ultrasound (first-line for biliary obstruction)
CT abdomen with contrast for mass/obstruction*
MRCP referral for biliary tree detail*
Further imaging per cause* if indicated
meds: IV crystalloid resuscitation if septic/hypovolemic
Piperacillin-tazobactam 3.375 g IV for cholangitis*
Lactulose 20-30 g PO/PR for hepatic encephalopathy*
Vitamin K 10 mg IV if coagulopathic*
Antiemetic (ondansetron 4 mg IV) as needed* if indicated
disposition: Transfer/admit for cholangitis, acute liver failure, significant hemolysis
Cholangitis requires ERCP-capable facility
Discharge stable, well-appearing patients with outpatient workup
Document EMTALA stabilization before transfer
discharge: Avoid alcohol and hepatotoxic agents/acetaminophen limits
Return for fever, confusion, worsening jaundice, bleeding
Outpatient lab recheck arranged
Hepatology/GI referral

redFlags: Coagulopathy or signs of acute liver failure
compliance: EMTALA screening exam and stabilization documented before disposition
Cholangitis/acute liver failure require transfer to ERCP/hepatology/ICU-capable hospital with accepting physician
Report suspected acute viral hepatitis (A/B/C) to Texas health authorities per reporting rules
Texas FSED fee/insurance disclosure; observation acceptable only if stable and well-appearing

name: Anorectal Pain
summary: Usually benign (hemorrhoids, fissure) but must exclude perirectal/perianal abscess, especially in diabetics/immunocompromised.

triage: Vitals incl. temperature; screen for fever
Pain character, bleeding, discharge, bowel habits
Diabetes/immunocompromise (necrotizing risk)
Bowel/bladder dysfunction (deep abscess)
Notify MD for fever or systemic toxicity
nursing: Position for perianal exam; provide privacy/chaperone
Notify MD for crepitus or rapidly spreading erythema
keyOrders: IV access if procedure or systemic illness*
labs: Coagulation panel if anticoagulated* if indicated
imaging: None for uncomplicated external disease
CT/MRI pelvis for suspected deep/complex perirectal abscess*
Bedside US to localize superficial abscess*
Imaging if necrotizing infection suspected* if indicated
meds: Topical lidocaine and hydrocortisone for hemorrhoids/fissure
Stool softener (docusate 100 mg PO BID) + fiber
Nitroglycerin 0.4% or nifedipine topical for anal fissure*
Bedside I&D with local anesthesia for simple perianal abscess
Antibiotics (e.g., amoxicillin-clavulanate) if cellulitis/immunocompromised* if indicated
reassess: Reassess pain after topical therapy or I&D
disposition: Transfer/surgery for complex/deep abscess, necrotizing infection, immunocompromised
Discharge simple hemorrhoids/fissure and drained simple abscess
Observe if systemic signs evolving
Document EMTALA stabilization before transfer
discharge: Sitz baths, high-fiber diet, stool softeners
Wound care/packing instructions after I&D
Return for fever, spreading redness, increasing pain
Colorectal/surgery follow-up
followup: Surgery/colorectal for recurrent abscess or thrombosed hemorrhoids
Wound recheck in 24-48 h after I&D
GI/colonoscopy if bleeding unexplained or age >45
ER return for systemic infection signs

redFlags: Perirectal/deep abscess with fever or systemic toxicity
Immunocompromised or diabetic with perianal infection
compliance: EMTALA screening exam and stabilization documented before disposition
Necrotizing infection/complex abscess require emergent transfer to surgical hospital with accepting physician
Observation acceptable after simple I&D if patient stable and non-toxic
Texas FSED fee/insurance disclosure; document any procedure consent and transfer paperwork

name: Esophageal Food Impaction
summary: Food bolus lodged in esophagus; risk of airway compromise, aspiration, and perforation if prolonged or with sharp/disk objects.

triage: Vitals incl. SpO2; assess airway and drooling
Time of onset, complete vs partial obstruction, ability to swallow saliva
Underlying eosinophilic esophagitis, stricture, or rings
Foreign body type (bone, disk battery concern)
Immediate MD notification for airway distress or inability to handle secretions
nursing: Continuous pulse oximetry and frequent reassessment
keyOrders: 12-lead ECG before glucagon if cardiac history*
labs: Lactate if perforation suspected* if indicated
imaging: Avoid barium (impairs endoscopy and aspiration risk)
Soft-tissue neck and chest X-ray for radiopaque object/free air*
CT neck/chest if perforation or sharp object suspected*
Endoscopy is both diagnostic and therapeutic - arrange/transfer
meds: Glucagon 1-2 mg IV (may relax LES) - limited efficacy*
Ondansetron 4 mg IV for nausea before glucagon
IV fluids for hydration while NPO
Avoid meat tenderizer/papain (perforation risk)
Procedural sedation meds per endoscopy setting* if indicated
reassess: Reassess airway and secretion handling continuously
Determine spontaneous passage vs persistent impaction
disposition: Transfer to endoscopy-capable hospital if not resolved (within ~6-12 h, sooner if complete)
Emergent transfer if airway compromise or suspected perforation
Discharge only after confirmed passage and tolerating PO
Document EMTALA stabilization before transfer
discharge: Soft diet, thorough chewing, avoid large meat boluses
Return for recurrent impaction, chest pain, fever, inability to swallow
Counsel on underlying esophageal disease evaluation
Arrange GI follow-up
followup: GI for EGD, biopsy (eosinophilic esophagitis), stricture dilation
ER return for recurrent or persistent symptoms

redFlags: Inability to handle secretions / drooling (airway risk)
Disk battery or sharp object impaction (emergent)
Complete obstruction persisting beyond several hours
compliance: EMTALA screening exam and stabilization documented before transfer
Unresolved impaction requires timely transfer to endoscopy-capable hospital with accepting physician (urgent if complete/airway risk)
FSED observation acceptable only briefly while monitoring for spontaneous passage in a stable patient
Texas FSED fee/insurance disclosure; document transfer mode and physician-to-physician communication
